text: Before the internet, reading was understood as a technology of interiority. The printed page demanded that the reader's attention move inward — following an argument, inhabiting a character's consciousness, holding narrative threads across hundreds of pages. This was not a natural capacity; it was one cultivated over centuries of print culture. Neuroscientist Maryanne Wolf calls this 'deep reading,' a set of cognitive skills that includes inference, critical analysis, and empathic projection. These skills, she warns, are not hardwired. They depend on repeated practice, and they can be lost. A generation raised on screen-skimming may inherit the alphabet while losing the deeper literacy it once supported.
question: According to neuroscientist Maryanne Wolf, which cognitive skill is included in what she calls 'deep reading'?

options.text: Rapid pattern recognition across visual fields
